articles.title: Show HN: Tiny-vLLM – high performance LLM inference engine in C++ and CUDA
articles.summary: Tiny-vLLM is a high-performance LLM inference engine built in C++ and CUDA that allows developers to build their own models using smaller versions of vLLM. This repository provides the full source code for the inference server and offers a course on implementing the engine as a learning tool or teaching resource. The key technology here is the use of floating-point numbers, particularly bfloat16, which enables high-performance computations on GPUs. By leveraging CUDA kernels and KV (Key Value) cache, developers can achieve significant speedups in LLM forward passes.

articles.title: CVE-2026-48710: A Maintainer's Perspective
articles.summary: The CVE-2026-48710 vulnerability affects applications using Starlette's routing feature, which incorrectly reconstructs the request URL by concatenating the host with the path. This can lead to unauthorized access if a middleware uses the reconstructed URL to guard a route. The issue arises from an application pattern built on top of Starlette that assumes the request's path and host are always correct, whereas in reality they may be malformed or different from what was expected.

articles.title: Bijou64: A variable-length integer encoding
articles.summary: Bijou64 is a variable-length integer encoding that was developed for the Subduction CRDT sync protocol, intended to fix a signature-verification bug by representing each number only once. The design constraint of this encoding made it run faster than other common varint designs like LEB128. Bijou64 encodes a number as a sequence of 7-bit segments with continuation bits, allowing for efficient representation of small numbers and avoiding the need to write 8 bytes when representing a small number. However, its canonicalization mechanism can lead to problems if not implemented correctly, requiring additional validation during decoding.

articles.title: Leaving performance on the table
articles.summary: Leaving performance on the table is a common pitfall in optimizing binaries, as compilers assume every branch is equally taken unless specific hints are provided. By collecting profiles via perf over time and creating an optimized binary based on statistical occurrence of call graphs, developers can significantly improve code performance. This approach allows for more accurate optimization than instrumented binaries that rely solely on compiler flags.
